overall_summary: Terraflux, a small independent power producer, broadly supports the DOE ANOPR principles for large-load interconnection reform. Its main departures are: deposit and penalty requirements should not mirror Order 2023 amounts because those rules favor large, well-capitalized developers and crowd out smaller players; curtailment definitions for expedited studies must be operationally clear and financeable; and FERC or DOE should address the interconnection engineer workforce shortage that will worsen as reform adds study volume.

quotes.text: We agree with the first principle's assertion that FERC's jurisdiction should be limited to interconnections directly to transmission facilities, consistent with FERC's seven-factor test.
quotes.concern: Agrees that FERC jurisdiction should cover only direct transmission interconnections under the seven-factor test

quotes.text: We agree with the second principle's assertion that reforms should apply to new loads greater than 20MW and new hybrid facilities greater than 20MW, aligned with FERC's generation thresholds of the LGIP
quotes.concern: Agrees with the 20MW threshold aligned to LGIP generation thresholds

quotes.text: We agree with the third principle's assertion that both co-located and near-located load plus generation facilities should be studied together as hybrid facilities, rather than forced to be studied as one separate load interconnection request and one separate generation interconnection request. We propose that 'near-located' means within three busbar substations of each other, while 'co-located' means within the same busbar substation.
quotes.concern: Supports joint study of co-located and near-located hybrid facilities and proposes a three-busbar definition of near-located

quotes.text: FERC should not set load interconnection requirements to exactly mirror those the amounts established in Order No. 2023. The financial and commercial requirements set in the generator interconnection reform process, although successful in lowering the number of generation interconnection requests to be studied, have dissuaded inventive but less-resourced generation interconnection customers from being studied in the queues.
quotes.concern: Opposes copying Order 2023 deposit amounts because they disadvantage smaller, less-capitalized developers

quotes.text: The reformed generation procedures favor large balance sheet and often less-nimble interconnection customers to crowd-out and morph generator interconnection queues into oligopolies.
quotes.concern: Order 2023 financial requirements have concentrated queue access among large-balance-sheet developers

quotes.text: FERC should consider alternative ways to deter load interconnection requests which have no intention of being built, rather than imposing significant entry taxes and exit taxes for load interconnection customers to be studied by the grid operator.
quotes.concern: Urges FERC to find deterrence mechanisms other than high financial barriers

quotes.text: FERC should allow the interconnection customer to indicate what those net amounts will be, rather than the grid operator assuming full nameplate of load and full nameplate of generation is netted from one another. Secondly, FERC should allow the customer to provide two additional, alternative 'net amounts scenarios' to also be studied, concurrent with the first scenario, which would allow the customer to right-size its hybrid facility components between facility needs and grid capacity.
quotes.concern: Customers should define their own net injection/withdrawal amounts and be allowed two alternative scenarios studied concurrently

quotes.text: While we agree with the seventh principle's assertion that large load and hybrid facilities which agree to be curtailable should be expedited in a 60-day study duration, the details around curtailment allowances should be clearly and reasonably defined by FERC, such that a facility's "high curtailment scenario" can be still be operationally understood, modeled, and financed against by the interconnection customer.
quotes.concern: Supports expedited 60-day studies for curtailable loads but requires clear, financeable curtailment definitions

quotes.text: We agree with the eighth principle's assertion that 100% of assigned network upgrades costs should be financed by the interconnection customer, however a liquid reimbursement mechanism is a cleaner and simpler approach than reimbursement crediting via transmission expenses.
quotes.concern: Agrees on full cost assignment but prefers direct cash reimbursement over transmission credit mechanisms

quotes.text: FERC or the DOE should consider if the resources of the federal government can be used to alleviate a major bottleneck of load interconnections nationwide: the shortage and overburdened conditions of the electrical engineers who perform the interconnection studies themselves. Higher compensating opportunities at grid developers and private consultancies have attracted these specialists away, and staff shortages is partly the cause of the protracted study turnaround times, which continue even after Order No. 2023 implementation.
quotes.concern: Asks FERC and DOE to address the engineer shortage driving slow study turnaround times

quotes.text: Load interconnection reform, though absolutely necessary, will further exacerbate the bottleneck. We urge FERC or DOE to consider offering monetary or non-monetary support towards these specialized interconnection engineers performing interconnection studies, to retain existing and also attract new/returning engineers to this critical function.
quotes.concern: Reform will worsen the engineer shortage; urges FERC or DOE to offer financial or other support to retain and recruit study engineers

bins.name: FERC jurisdiction scope
bins.description: Terraflux agrees that FERC jurisdiction should be limited to direct transmission interconnections under the seven-factor test.

bins.name: 20MW applicability threshold
bins.description: Terraflux supports applying the reforms to loads and hybrid facilities above 20MW, consistent with LGIP generation thresholds.

bins.name: Joint hybrid study requirement
bins.description: Terraflux agrees that co-located and near-located load-plus-generation facilities should be studied together as a single hybrid request rather than split into separate load and generation studies.

bins.name: Deposits and penalties design
bins.description: Terraflux agrees that deterrence mechanisms are appropriate but opposes mirroring Order 2023 financial amounts. It argues those amounts already concentrate queue access among large developers, and urges FERC to find alternative deterrence tools that do not act as entry and exit taxes.

bins.name: Customer-defined net amounts
bins.description: Terraflux argues that interconnection customers, not grid operators, should define the net injection and withdrawal amounts to be studied, and should be able to submit two additional alternative scenarios concurrently to right-size hybrid components.

bins.name: System protection requirements
bins.description: Terraflux agrees that hybrid interconnections should be required to install system protections.

bins.name: Expedited study curtailment terms
bins.description: Terraflux supports the 60-day expedited study for curtailable facilities but says curtailment terms must be defined clearly enough for the interconnection customer to model and finance against the high curtailment scenario.

bins.name: Upgrade cost assignment method
bins.description: Terraflux agrees that interconnection customers should finance 100% of assigned network upgrade costs but prefers a direct cash reimbursement mechanism over crediting through transmission expenses.

bins.name: Study process clarity
bins.description: Terraflux supports expedited studies for curtailable loads but requires that curtailment parameters be clearly and operationally defined so the facility can be modeled and financed.

bins.name: Cost responsibility and reimbursement
bins.description: Terraflux supports full cost responsibility for interconnection customers and prefers a liquid, direct reimbursement mechanism over transmission expense crediting.

bins.name: Co-located hybrid treatment
bins.description: Terraflux supports treating co-located and near-located load-plus-generation pairs as hybrid facilities studied together.

bins.name: Flexible net amount scenarios
bins.description: Terraflux wants interconnection customers to define their own net amounts and to submit two alternative net-amount scenarios studied concurrently, enabling right-sizing of hybrid facility components.

bins.name: Near-located definition
bins.description: Terraflux proposes that near-located means within three busbar substations, distinguishing it from co-located which means the same busbar substation.

bins.name: Interconnection engineer shortage
bins.description: Terraflux identifies the shortage of interconnection study engineers as a nationwide bottleneck that reform will worsen. It urges FERC or DOE to offer monetary or non-monetary support to retain existing engineers and attract new ones to this function.
